title: Anglicised dhansak
subtitle: Lamb, lentil, and squash curry with tamarind
excerpt: Lamb shoulder braised with red lentils, butternut squash, and tomato in a spiced onion base, soured with tamarind. The Anglo-Indian adaptation of the Parsi dhansak — richer and milder than the original, but keeping its defining combination of meat, lentil, and sweet-sour finish.
sourceNotes: Based on the Parsi dhansak tradition as adapted through Anglo-Indian cooking, documented in Niloufer Ichaporia King, 'My Bombay Kitchen' (2007) and Lizzie Collingham, 'Curry: A Tale of Cooks and Conquerors' (2005). The Parsi original uses a specific blend of dhansak masala; this version uses curry powder and garam masala as practical substitutes for the Anglo-Indian kitchen.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well. The lentils thicken further on freezing; add stock when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well in a larger pot.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day. Reheat gently with a splash of water.

content.text: Dhansak is a Parsi dish, one of the great dishes of the Parsi community of western India descended from Zoroastrian immigrants from Iran, and it arrived in Anglo-Indian cooking as a prestige curry, a step above the everyday. The defining combination is meat braised alongside lentils and vegetables until everything collapses together, then sharpened with tamarind. The Parsi version uses a specific spice blend (dhansak masala) and up to four types of lentil; this adaptation uses curry powder and red lentils, trading some depth for accessibility.

The tamarind is the flavour that distinguishes dhansak from other lentil curries. Use tamarind paste rather than tamarind concentrate — the paste has more complexity. It goes in near the end and should be tasted carefully: the right amount of sourness is personal, but the dish should have a clear sweet-sour finish.
Brown the lamb and build the base

Heat {{sunflower-oil}} of oil in a Dutch oven over a high heat. Brown {{lamb-shoulder}} of lamb shoulder in batches and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium, add {{onion}} sliced onion, and cook for 10 minutes until golden. Add {{garlic}} garlic, {{ginger-fresh}} ginger, {{curry-powder}} of curry powder, and {{turmeric-ground}} of turmeric. Stir for 2 minutes.

Return the lamb to the pot. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{red-lentils}} of rinsed lentils, {{butternut-squash}} of squash, and {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to the boil, cover, and simmer on a low heat for 60–70 minutes until the lamb is tender and the lentils have dissolved into the sauce. Add {{tamarind-paste}} of tamarind paste, stir, and taste — add more if you want more sourness. Stir in {{garam-masala}} of garam masala, rest for 10 minutes, and scatter {{coriander-fresh}} fresh coriander to serve.

items.symptom: Curry is too thick and porridge-like
items.fix: Add more stock or water and stir to loosen. Dhansak should be thick but sponable, not stiff.
Start with 1 tablespoon and taste before adding more. The sourness intensifies after resting.

items.cause: Temperature too low for the cut, or cut too large
items.fix: Shoulder at 4 cm cubes needs a proper simmer. Increase heat slightly and add a splash of water if needed.

content.text: Dhansak is Parsi food first: a cornerstone of Zoroastrian cuisine in western India, eaten at celebrations and family gatherings, always with brown rice and caramelised onions. It entered the Anglo-Indian table during the colonial period, when Parsi merchants and professionals occupied a prominent position in Bombay society and their cooking became part of the shared food culture of the city. In British restaurants it has long occupied the space between a rogan josh and a balti, the dish for those who want complexity alongside the lentil body, though the restaurant version rarely matches the richness of the home-cooked original.
